author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm V

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The title of the Psalm is, “For her who receiveth the inheritance.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Church then is signified, who receiveth for her inheritance eternal life through our Lord Jesus Christ; that she may possess God Himself, in cleaving to whom she may be blessed, according to that, “Blessed are the meek, for they shall possess the earth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What earth, but that of which it is said, “Thou art my hope, my portion in the land of the living”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And again more clearly, “The Lord is the portion of mine inheritance and of my cup.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And conversely the word Church is said to be God’s inheritance according to that, “Ask of Me, and I shall give thee the heathen for thine inheritance.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore is God said to be our inheritance, because He feedeth and sustaineth us: and we are said to be God’s inheritance, because He ordereth and ruleth us.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore it is the voice of the Church in this Psalm called to her inheritance, that she too may herself become the inheritance of the Lord.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Hear my words, O Lord” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Being called she calleth upon the Lord; that the same Lord being her helper, she may pass through the wickedness of this world, and attain unto Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Understand my cry.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Psalmist well shows what this cry is; how from within, from the chamber of the heart, without the body’s utterance, it reaches unto God: for the bodily voice is heard, but the spiritual is understood.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Although this too may be God’s hearing, not with carnal ear, but in the omnipresence of His Majesty.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Attend Thou to the voice of my supplication;” that is, to that voice, which he maketh request that God would understand: of which what the nature is, he hath already intimated, when he said, “Understand my cry.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Attend Thou to the voice of my supplication, my King, and my God” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Although both the Son is God, and the Father God, and the Father and the Son together One God; and if asked of the Holy Ghost, we must give no other answer than that He is God; and when the Father, and the Son, and the Holy Ghost are mentioned together, we must understand nothing else, than One God; nevertheless Scripture is wont to give the appellation of King to the Son.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: According then to that which is said, “By Me man cometh to the Father,” rightly is it first, “my King;” and then, “my God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And yet has not the Psalmist said, Attend Ye; but, “Attend Thou.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the Catholic faith preaches not two or three Gods, but the Very Trinity, One God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Not that the same Trinity can be together, now the Father, now the Son, now the Holy Ghost, as Sabellius believed: but that the Father must be none but the Father, and the Son none but the Son, and the Holy Ghost none but the Holy Ghost, and this Trinity but One God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence when the Apostle had said, “Of whom are all things, by whom are all things, in whom are all things,” he is believed to have conveyed an intimation of the Very Trinity; and yet he did not add, to Them be glory; but, “to Him be glory.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Because I will pray unto Thee (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: O Lord, in the morning Thou wilt hear my voice.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What does that, which he said above, “Hear Thou,” mean, as if he desired to be heard immediately?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But now he saith, “in the morning Thou wilt hear;” not, hear Thou: and, “I will pray unto Thee;” not, I do pray unto Thee: and, as follows, “in the morning I will stand by Thee, and will see;” not, I do stand by Thee, and do see.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Unless perhaps his former prayer marks the invocation itself: but being in darkness amidst the storms of this world, he perceives that he does not see what he desires, and yet does not cease to hope, “For hope that is seen, is not hope.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Nevertheless, he understands why he does not see, because the night is not yet past, that is, the darkness which our sins have merited.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He says therefore, “Because I will pray unto Thee, O Lord;” that is, because Thou art so mighty to whom I shall make my prayer, “in the morning Thou wilt hear my voice.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thou art not He, he says, that can be seen by those, from whose eyes the night of sins is not yet withdrawn: when the night then of my error is past, and the darkness gone, which by my sins I have brought upon myself, then “Thou wilt hear my voice.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why then did he say above not, “Thou wilt hear,” but “hear Thou”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is it that after the Church cried out, “hear Thou,” and was not heard, she perceived what must needs pass away to enable her to be heard?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or is it that she was heard above, but doth not yet understand that she was heard, because she doth not yet see by whom she hath been heard; and what she now says, “In the morning Thou wilt hear,” she would have thus taken, In the morning I shall understand that I have been heard?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Such is that expression, “Arise, O Lord,” that is, make me arise.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But this latter is taken of Christ’s resurrection: but at all events that Scripture, “The Lord your God proveth you, that He may know whether ye love Him,” cannot be taken in any other sense, than, that ye by Him may know, and that it may be made evident to yourselves, what progress ye have made in His love.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “In the morning I will stand by Thee, and will see” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What is, “I will stand,” but “I will not lie down”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now what else is, to lie down, but to take rest on the earth, which is a seeking happiness in earthly pleasures?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I will stand by,” he says, “and will see.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We must not then cleave to things earthly, if we would see God, who is beheld by a clean heart.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For Thou art not a God who hast pleasure in iniquity.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The malignant man shall not dwell near Thee, nor shall the unrighteous abide before Thine eyes.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thou hast hated all that work iniquity, Thou wilt destroy all that speak a lie.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The man of blood, and the crafty man, the Lord will abominate” (vers.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Iniquity, malignity, lying, homicide, craft, and all the like, are the night of which we speak: on the passing away of which, the morning dawns, that God may be seen.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He has unfolded the reason, then, why he will stand by in the morning, and see: “For,” he says, “Thou art not a God who hast pleasure in iniquity.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For if He were a God who had pleasure in iniquity, He could be seen even by the iniquitous, so that He would not be seen in the morning, that is, when the night of iniquity is over.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The malignant man shall not dwell near Thee:” that is, he shall not so see, as to cleave to Thee.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence follows, “Nor shall the unrighteous abide before Thine eyes.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For their eyes, that is, their mind is beaten back by the light of truth, because of the darkness of their sins; by the habitual practice of which they are not able to sustain the brightness of right understanding.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore even they who see sometimes, that is, who understand the truth, are yet still unrighteous, they abide not therein through love of those things, which turn away from the truth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For they carry about with them their night, that is, not only the habit, but even the love, of sinning.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if this night shall pass away, that is, if they shall cease to sin, and this love and habit thereof be put to flight, the morning dawns, so that they not only understand, but also cleave to the truth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Thou hast hated all that work iniquity.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: God’s hatred may be understood from that form of expression, by which every sinner hates the truth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it seems that she too hates those, whom she suffers not to abide in her.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now they do not abide, who cannot bear the truth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Thou wilt destroy all that speak a lie.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this is the opposite to truth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But lest any one should suppose that any substance or nature is opposite to truth, let him understand that “a lie” has relation to that which is not, not to that which is.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For if that which is be spoken, truth is spoken: but if that which is not be spoken, it is a lie.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore saith he, “Thou wilt destroy all that speak a lie;” because drawing back from that which is, they turn aside to that which is not.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Many lies indeed seem to be for some one’s safety or advantage, spoken not in malice, but in kindness: such was that of those midwives in Exodus, who gave a false report to Pharaoh, to the end that the infants of the children of Israel might not be slain.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But even these are praised not for the fact, but for the disposition shown; since those who only lie in this way, will attain in time to a freedom from all lying.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in those that are perfect, not even these lies are found.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For to these it is said, “Let there be in your mouth, yea, yea; nay, nay; whatsoever is more, is of evil.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Nor is it without reason written in another place, “The mouth that lieth slayeth the soul:” lest any should imagine that the perfect and spiritual man ought to lie for this temporal life, in the death of which no soul is slain, neither his own, nor another’s.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But since it is one thing to lie, another to conceal the truth (if indeed it be one thing to say what is false, another not to say what is true), if haply one does not wish to give a man up even to this visible death, he should be prepared to conceal what is true, not to say what is false; so that he may neither give him up, nor yet lie, lest he slay his own soul for another’s body.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if he cannot yet do this, let him at all events admit only lies of such necessity, that he may attain to be freed even from these, if they alone remain, and receive the strength of the Holy Ghost, whereby he may despise all that must be suffered for the truth’s sake.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In fine, there are two kinds of lies, in which there is no great fault, and yet they are not without fault, either when we are in jest, or when we lie that we may do good.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That first kind, in jest, is for this reason not very hurtful, because there is no deception.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he to whom it is said knows that it is said for the sake of the jest.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But the second kind is for this reason the more inoffensive, because it carries with it some kindly intention.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And to say truth, that which has no duplicity, cannot even be called a lie.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As if, for example, a sword be intrusted to any one, and he promises to return it, when he who intrusted it to him shall demand it: if he chance to require his sword when in a fit of madness, it is clear it must not be returned then, lest he kill either himself or others, until soundness of mind be restored to him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Here then is no duplicity, because he, to whom the sword was intrusted, when he promised that he would return it at the other’s demand, did not imagine that he could require it when in a fit of madness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But even the Lord concealed the truth, when He said to the disciples, not yet strong enough, “I have many things to say unto you, but ye cannot bear them now:” and the Apostle Paul when he said, “I could not speak unto you as unto spiritual, but as unto carnal.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence it is clear that it is not blamable, sometimes not to speak what is true.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But to say what is false is not found to have been allowed to the perfect.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The man of blood, and the crafty man, the Lord will abominate.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What he said above, “Thou hast hated all that work iniquity, Thou wilt destroy all that speak a lie,” may well seem to be repeated here: so that one may refer “the man of blood” to “the worker of iniquity,” and “the crafty man” to the “lie.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is craft, when one thing is done, another pretended.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He used an apt word too, when he said, “will abominate.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the disinherited are usually called abominated.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now this Psalm is, “for her who receiveth the inheritance;” and she adds the exulting joy of her hope, in saying, “But I, in the multitude of Thy mercy, will enter into Thine house” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “In the multitude of mercy:” perhaps he means in the multitude of perfected and blessed men, of whom that city shall consist, of which the Church is now in travail, and is bearing few by few.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now that many men regenerated and perfected, are rightly called the multitude of God’s mercy, who can deny; when it is most truly said, “What is man that Thou art mindful of him, or the son of man that Thou visitest him?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I will enter into Thine house:” as a stone into a building, I suppose, is the meaning.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what else is the house of God than the Temple of God, of which it is said, “for the temple of God is holy, which temple ye are”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Of which building He is the cornerstone, whom the Power and Wisdom of God coeternal with the Father assumed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I will worship at Thy holy temple, in Thy fear.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “At the temple,” we understand as, “near” the temple.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he does not say, I will worship “in” Thy holy temple; but, “I will worship at Thy holy temple.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It must be understood too to be spoken not of perfection, but of progress toward perfection: so that the words, “I will enter into Thine house,” should signify perfection.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But that this may come to a happy issue, “I will” first, he says, “worship at Thy holy temple.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And perhaps on this account he added, “in Thy fear;” which is a great defence to those that are advancing toward salvation.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But when any one shall have arrived there, in him comes to pass that which is written, “perfect love casteth out fear.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For they do not fear Him who is now their friend, to whom it is said, “henceforth I will not call you servants, but friends,” when they have been brought through to that which was promised.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “O Lord, lead me forth in Thy justice because of mine enemies” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He has here sufficiently plainly declared that he is on his onward road, that is, in progress toward perfection, not yet in perfection itself, when he desires eagerly that he may be led forth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But, “in Thy justice,” not in that which seems so to men.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For to return evil for evil seems justice: but it is not His justice of whom it is said, “He maketh His sun to rise on the good and on the evil:” for even when God punishes sinners, He does not inflict His evil on them, but leaves them to their own evil.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Behold,” the Psalmist says, “he travailed with injustice, he hath conceived toil, and brought forth iniquity: he hath opened a ditch, and digged it, and hath fallen into the pit which he wrought: his pains shall be turned on his own head, and his iniquity shall descend on his own pate.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When then God punishes, He punishes as a judge those that transgress the law, not by bringing evil upon them from Himself, but driving them on to that which they have chosen, to fill up the sum of their misery.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But man, when he returns evil for evil, does it with an evil will: and on this account is himself first evil, when he would punish evil.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Direct in Thy sight my way.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Nothing is clearer, than that he here sets forth that time, in which he is journeying onward.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this is a way which is traversed not in any regions of the earth, but in the affections of the heart.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “In Thy sight,” he says, “direct my way:” that is, where no man sees; who are not to be trusted in their praise or blame.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For they can in no wise judge of another man’s conscience, wherein the way toward God is traversed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence it is added, “for truth is not in their mouth” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To whose judgment of course then there is no trusting, and therefore must we fly within to conscience, and the sight of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Their heart is vain.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How then can truth be in their mouth, whose heart is deceived by sin, and the punishment of sin?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence men are called back by that voice, “Wherefore do ye love vanity, and seek a lie?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Their throat is an open sepulchre.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It may be referred to signify gluttony, for the sake of which men very often lie by flattery.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And admirably has he said, “an open sepulchre:” for this gluttony is ever gaping with open mouth, not as sepulchres, which, on the reception of corpses, are closed up.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This also may be understood hereby, that with lying and blind flattery men draw to themselves those whom they entice to sin; and as it were devour them, when they turn them to their own way of living.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And when this happens to them, since by sin they die, those by whom they are led along, are rightly called open sepulchres: for themselves too are in a manner lifeless, being destitute of the life of truth; and they take in to themselves dead men, whom having slain by lying words and a vain heart, they turn unto themselves.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “With their own tongues they dealt craftily:” that is, with evil tongues.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this seems to be signified, when he says “their own.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the evil have evil tongues, that is, they speak evil, when they speak craftily.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To whom the Lord saith, “How can ye, being evil, speak good things?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Judge them, O God: let them fall from their own thoughts” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is a prophecy, not a curse.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he does not wish that it should come to pass; but he perceives what will come to pass.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this happens to them, not because he appears to have wished for it, but because they are such as to deserve that it should happen.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For so also what he says afterwards, “Let all that hope in Thee rejoice,” he says by way of prophecy; since he perceives that they will rejoice.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Likewise is it said prophetically, “Stir up Thy strength, and come:” for he saw that He would come.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Although the words, “Let them fall from their own thoughts,” may be taken thus also, that it may rather be believed to be a wish for their good by the Psalmist, whilst they fall from their evil thoughts, that is, that they may no more think evil.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what follows, “drive them out,” forbids this interpretation.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it can in no wise be taken in a favourable sense, that one is driven out by God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore it is understood to be said prophetically, and not of ill will; when this is said, which must necessarily happen to such as chose to persevere in those sins, which have been mentioned.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let them,” therefore, “fall from their own thoughts,” is, let them fall by their self-accusing thoughts, “their own conscience also bearing witness,” as the Apostle says, “and their thoughts accusing or excusing, in the revelation of the just judgment of God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “According to the multitude of their ungodlinesses drive them out:” that is, drive them out far away.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this is “according to the multitude of their ungodlinesses,” that they should be driven out far away.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The ungodly then are driven out from that inheritance, which is possessed by knowing and seeing God: as diseased eyes are driven out from the shining of the light, when what is gladness to others is pain to them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore these shall not stand in the morning, and see.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And that expression is as great a punishment, as that which is said, “But for me it is good to cleave to the Lord,” is a great reward.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To this punishment is opposed, “Enter thou into the joy of Thy Lord;” for similar to this expulsion is, “Cast him into outer darkness.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Since they have embittered Thee, O Lord: I am,” saith He, “the Bread which came down from heaven;” again, “Labour for the meat which wasteth not;” again, “Taste and see that the Lord is sweet.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But to sinners the bread of truth is bitter.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence they hate the mouth of him that speaketh the truth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These then have embittered God, who by sin have fallen into such a state of sickliness, that the food of truth, in which healthy souls delight, as if it were bitter as gall, they cannot bear.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And let all rejoice that hope in Thee;” those of course to whose taste the Lord is sweet.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “They will exult for evermore, and Thou wilt dwell in them” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This will be the exultation for evermore, when the just become the Temple of God, and He, their Indweller, will be their joy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And all that love Thy name shall glory in Thee:” as when what they love is present for them to enjoy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And well is it said, “in Thee,” as if in possession of the inheritance, of which the title of the Psalm speaks: when they too are His inheritance, which is intimated by, “Thou wilt dwell in them.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: From which good they are kept back, whom God, according to the multitude of their ungodlinesses, driveth out.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For Thou wilt bless the just man” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is blessing, to glory in God, and to be inhabited by God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Such sanctification is given to the just.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But that they may be justified, a calling goes before: which is not of merit, but of the grace of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For all have sinned, and want the glory of God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For whom He called, them He also justified; and whom He justified, them He also glorified.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Since then calling is not of our merit, but of the goodness and mercy of God, he went on to say, “O Lord, as with the shield of Thy good will Thou hast crowned us.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For God’s good will goes before our good will, to call sinners to repentance.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And these are the arms whereby the enemy is overcome, against whom it is said, “Who will bring accusation against God’s elect?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Again, “if God be for us, who can be against us?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Who spared not His Only Son, but delivered Him up for us all.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For if, when we were enemies, Christ died for us; much more being reconciled shall we be saved from wrath through Him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is that unconquerable shield, whereby the enemy is driven back, when he suggests despair of our salvation through the multitude of tribulations and temptations.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The whole contents of the Psalm, then, are a prayer that she may be heard, from the words, “hear my words, O Lord,” unto, “my King, and my God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Then follows a view of those things which hinder the sight of God, that is, a knowledge that she is heard, from the words, “because I shall pray unto Thee, O Lord, in the morning Thou wilt hear my voice,” unto, “the man of blood and the crafty man the Lord will abominate.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thirdly, she hopes that she, who is to be the house of God, even now begins to draw near to Him in fear, before that perfection which casteth out fear, from the words, “but I in the multitude of Thy mercy,” unto, “I will worship at Thy holy temple in Thy fear.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Fourthly, as she is progressing and advancing amongst those very things which she feels to hinder her, she prays that she may be assisted within, where no man seeth, lest she be turned aside by evil tongues, for the words, “O Lord, lead me forth in Thy justice because of my enemies,” unto, “with their tongues they dealt craftily.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Fifthly, is a prophecy of what punishment awaits the ungodly, when the just man shall scarcely be saved; and of what reward the just shall obtain, who, when they were called, came, and bore all things manfully, till they were brought to the end, from the words, “judge them, O God,” unto the end of the Psalm.
